text: Cardiorespiratory fitness non-response to exercise training is dose-dependent, with non-response rates of 69%, 40%, 29%, 0%, and 0% for individuals training 60, 120, 180, 240, or 300 minutes per week respectively over 6 weeks.
Adding two additional 60-minute exercise sessions per week to a training program universally eliminates cardiorespiratory fitness non-response in all previously classified non-responders.
There are no true non-responders to exercise training; apparent non-response is eliminated when exercise dose is sufficiently increased.
The magnitude of cardiorespiratory fitness improvement from exercise training is primarily determined by changes in total haemoglobin mass, which is the strongest independent determinant of VO2max.
specific_value: rpartial = 0.49 for haemoglobin mass predicting VO2max

text: Training at only 60 minutes per week for 6 weeks does not produce a statistically significant increase in maximal exercise power output, while 120+ minutes per week does.
A minimum of 240 minutes per week of endurance training over 6 weeks is needed to achieve zero non-responders in a group of healthy adults.
Changes in VO2max strongly and independently predict maximal exercise power output response to training, with a partial correlation coefficient of 0.74 or greater.
